name: OSPF
summary: Open-standard link-state IGP. Every router in an area floods LSAs describing its directly connected links, builds an identical Link-State Database (LSDB), and runs Dijkstra's SPF algorithm to compute the shortest path tree from itself to every other destination.
howItWorks: Routers form adjacencies via Hello packets, exchange Database Description packets to sync the LSDB, flood LSAs whenever a link state changes, and re-run SPF to recompute the tree. Areas (especially area 0, the backbone) limit flooding scope and SPF cost so the protocol scales beyond a flat topology.
strengths: Open standard — runs on every vendor
Fast convergence after a topology change
weaknesses: CPU and memory intensive on large flat areas
Area design matters — bad design hurts convergence

name: EIGRP
metric: Composite — bandwidth + delay (load, reliability, MTU available)
summary: Cisco-developed (open since 2013) advanced distance-vector IGP that uses DUAL — the Diffusing Update Algorithm — to guarantee loop-free paths. Maintains a successor (best path) and a feasible successor (pre-computed backup) per destination for instant failover.
howItWorks: Hello packets establish neighbour relationships; the full topology table is exchanged once; then only partial, triggered updates flow on change (carried over the reliable Reliable Transport Protocol). On a link failure, the router switches to its feasible successor instantly or, if none exists, queries neighbours for a new loop-free path.
strengths: Low overhead in steady state (no periodic full updates)
Simple to configure compared to OSPF
weaknesses: Adoption is largely Cisco-only despite being open
No area concept — large topologies need careful summarisation
Composite metric is opaque; troubleshooting requires understanding the formula

name: BGP
metric: No single metric — attribute-based path selection (LOCAL_PREF, AS-PATH, MED, …)
summary: The routing protocol of the public internet. Path-vector protocol exchanging routes between Autonomous Systems — each route carries the complete AS-path it has traversed, making loop detection trivial. Path selection is rich and policy-driven, not metric-driven.
howItWorks: Establishes a TCP session (port 179) to each configured peer, exchanges its full table once (the global IPv4 table is ~950K routes), then sends incremental updates on change. Best-path selection runs the BGP decision process: LOCAL_PREF wins → shortest AS-PATH → lowest origin code → lowest MED → eBGP over iBGP → lowest IGP cost → lowest router ID. The winner is installed and re-advertised with this AS prepended.
strengths: Built for internet scale (millions of prefixes)
Policy-rich — route filtering, communities, AS-PATH manipulation
Stable in steady state, well understood
Same protocol works for global routing and datacenter fabrics
weaknesses: Complex policy interactions can produce surprising path choices
Hijacks and route leaks impact global reachability — RPKI helps but isn't ubiquitous
iBGP requires full mesh or route reflectors

name: RIP
summary: Original distance-vector IGP. Routers broadcast or multicast their full routing table to neighbours every 30 seconds; neighbours increment each metric by one and pick the lowest. Simple, well-understood, and mostly of historical interest.
howItWorks: Periodic updates every 30 seconds — each neighbour receives a list of (network, metric) pairs, adds 1 to each metric, and selects the lowest as best. Triggered updates speed up change propagation; split horizon and route poisoning mitigate (but don't fully eliminate) count-to-infinity loops.
strengths: Implemented everywhere — works in any lab
weaknesses: 15-hop diameter limit prevents anything but small networks
Only metric is hop count — ignores bandwidth and delay
RIPv1 has no authentication and no VLSM support
